questions.q: What is the law of conservation of mass?
questions.answer: The law of conservation of mass states that matter cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
key_points: Mass is conserved in a chemical reaction

questions.answer: Mole fraction is the ratio of the number of moles of a component to the total number of moles of all components in a solution.
key_points: Mole fraction is a measure of the concentration of a solution

questions.answer: Raoult's law states that the partial pressure of a component in a solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction.
key_points: Raoult's law relates partial pressure and mole fraction

questions.q: What is the difference between a molecule and an atom?
questions.answer: A molecule is a group of two or more atoms chemically bonded together, whereas an atom is the smallest unit of a chemical element.
key_points: Atoms are the building blocks of molecules

questions.answer: Atomic mass unit (amu) is one-twelfth the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
key_points: Atomic mass unit is a standard unit of mass

questions.q: What is the significance of Thomson's oil drop experiment?
questions.answer: Thomson's oil drop experiment demonstrated that atoms are positively charged and have a negative charge in the form of electrons.
key_points: Thomson's experiment showed the presence of electrons in atoms

questions.answer: The Rutherford-Bohr atomic model consists of a small, dense nucleus surrounded by electrons in circular orbits. The electrons jump from one orbit to another by emitting or absorbing energy.
key_points: Rutherford-Bohr model explains atomic structure and electron jumps

questions.q: Explain the concept of atomic number and mass number.
questions.answer: Atomic number is the number of protons in an atom's nucleus, while mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.
key_points: Atomic number and mass number are important atomic properties

questions.q: Compare the characteristics of isotopes and isobars.
questions.answer: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different mass numbers, while isobars are atoms of different elements with the same mass number.
key_points: Isotopes and isobars differ in atomic number and element

questions.q: What is the concept of electron configuration?
questions.answer: Electron configuration is the arrangement of electrons in an atom's orbitals, following the Aufbau principle and Pauli's exclusion principle.
key_points: Electron configuration explains the arrangement of electrons in atoms

questions.q: Describe the Aufbau principle and Pauli's exclusion principle.
questions.answer: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill the lowest available energy levels, while Pauli's exclusion principle states that no two electrons can have the same set of quantum numbers.
key_points: Aufbau and Pauli's principles explain electron configuration

questions.q: Explain the concept of Hund's rule of maximum multiplicity.
questions.answer: Hund's rule states that when filling orbitals of equal energy, electrons occupy empty orbitals before pairing up in an orbital, resulting in the maximum multiplicity.
key_points: Hund's rule explains electron configuration in degenerate orbitals

questions.q: Describe the periodic table and its significance.
questions.answer: The periodic table is a tabular arrangement of elements based on their atomic numbers, electron configurations, and recurring chemical properties, which helps in predicting the properties of elements.
key_points: Periodic table explains periodic trends and element properties

questions.q: Compare the characteristics of s, p, d, and f orbitals.
questions.answer: s-orbitals are spherical, p-orbitals are dumbbell-shaped, d-orbitals have a four-leaf clover shape, and f-orbitals have a complex, multi-lobed shape.
key_points: Orbital shapes and types are important in electron configuration

questions.q: Explain the concept of atomic radius and its variation across the periodic table.
questions.answer: Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period and increases down a group in the periodic table, due to the increasing nuclear charge and electron shielding.
key_points: Atomic radius explains periodic trends in atomic size
